Zandpack package

Submodules

Zandpack.TimedependentTransport module

Zandpack.TimedependentTransport.AdaptiveDOP(f, sig0, psi0, omega0, eps, t0, t1, dH, delta_func, Ixi, h_guess=None, dH_given=True, print_to_file=True, fixed_mode=False, name='Runge-Kutta', write_func=None, print_step=50, plot=False, use_GPU=False, atol=None, rtol=None, elec_names=['left', 'right'])[source]
Zandpack.TimedependentTransport.AdaptiveRK4(f, sig0, psi0, omega0, eps, t0, t1, dH, delta_func, Ixi, h_guess=None, dH_given=True, print_to_file=True, fixed_mode=False, name='Runge-Kutta', write_func=None, print_step=50, plot=False, use_GPU=False, atol=None, rtol=None, elec_names=['left', 'right'])[source]
Zandpack.TimedependentTransport.J(PI_a)[source]
Zandpack.TimedependentTransport.Jk(PI_a)[source]
Zandpack.TimedependentTransport.PI(psi_a, ixi)
Zandpack.TimedependentTransport.PI_nb(psi_a, ixi)[source]
Zandpack.TimedependentTransport.PI_np(psi_a, ixi)[source]
Zandpack.TimedependentTransport.PI_opti(psi_a, ixi, psi_a_idx)[source]
Zandpack.TimedependentTransport.Q_nb(psi_a, ixi)[source]
Zandpack.TimedependentTransport.Q_np(psi_a, ixi, nonzero_idx=None)[source]

Returns (Pi + Pi^dagger )/hbar Fastest version written so far and is pretty simple.

Both psi_a and ixi is (nk,nx,noT,no) arrays

class Zandpack.TimedependentTransport.TD_Transport(elec_geoms, device_geom, mu_i=[0.0, 0.0], kT_i=[0.1, 0.1])[source]

Bases: object

Check_input_to_ODE(loose_fermi=True)[source]

Checks the result of the eigendecompositions are indeed consistent with the input. For testing purposes. If the ODECheck.txt contains large numbers (say >1e-4), increase the number of fermi poles included either in the beginning or using the modify_occupations commandline tool.

Electrodes(names=['EM', 'EP'], semi_infs=['-a1', '+a1'], kp=[[50, 1, 1], [50, 1, 1]], basis='SZ', pp_folder='../pp', overwrite=True, mpi='')[source]

Constructs the DFT calculator objects for the electrodes stripped down version, more flags could be added This routine is circumventable by assigning self.Device manually (see tutorials). If doing so, be sure to give the specify save_SE=True in the initialization of the device instance.

Fit(fact=2.0, Fallback_W=30.0, NumL=3, fit_mode='all', use_analytical_jac=True, min_method='SLSQP', ebounds=(-5, 5), wbounds=(0.01, 3.0), gbounds=(None, None), tol=-1.0, options={}, fit_real_part=False, specific_bounds=None, alpha_PO=0.0, cons='ascending', force_PSD=True, force_PSD_tol=0.0, which_e=None, which_k=None, init_E=None, init_G=None, exc_idx=None)[source]

Here the fitting of either the gammas or the self-energies are done (fit_mode = ‘all’ or ‘all_from_SE’) You can also rerun a fit, no need to delete previous fit. But you can delete a previous fit with a “reset_all_fits()” call. fact, together with NumL and ebounds, determines how large broadening is on the initial Lorentzian Fallback_W: is used when only one sampling point is used (redundant now) NumL: Number of Lorentzians used to fit use_analytical_jac: use analytical jacobian for minimization min_method: the method used by the minimize function. (SLSQP, L-BFGS-B, many more… see scipy docs on minimize) ebounds: limits on where the Lorentzian centers can be wbounds: limits on how broad or narrow the lorentzian centers can be gbounds: limits on the coeffcients tol: the cutoff on which matrix-elements are attempted fitted. The absolute value of over all energies just need to have one larger value than this to get fitted fit_real_part : if true only the real part of the Gamma/Selfenergy is fitted specific bounds: list

[ {(ik, iL):((B1, B2), (C1, C2)) ,…}, … ] of specific bounds on the lorentzian iL at point ik.

alpha_PO : (positive number) Adds a term that drives the Lorentzians away from each other cons : additional constraints on each lorentzian. force_PSD : forces the matrices with the coefficients for the Lorentzians to be positive semidefinite (or have eigenvalues larger than force_PSD_tol). force_PSD_tol: lower bound on how large the eigenvalues can be. float, (NumL,) array or (nk, NumL) array giving smallest eigenvalue for each fitting matrix. which_e: Which electrode you want to fit. For polishing individual fits. which_k: Which k-point you want to fit. For polishing individual fits. Note: This function behaves differently depending on if you have given the code a single energy-point, or multiple. If a single point is given, the code simply gives one Lorentzian with width Fallback_W, which takes the value of the sampled point at the apex if you give the code >1 point, the it instead fits the curve linearly interpolated between the points. Todo: Still needs to simultaneusly fit the Hilbert transform….

FitNO2O()[source]

Used e.g. when curvefit_all has been used, to write the new fitted gammas in the non-orthogonal basis to the gammas in the orthognalized basis

Gamma_info(lead=0, tol=0.001, ik=0, f=None, return_vals=False, only_upper=True)[source]

Useful function for fitting. Give the descendingly largest components of the gamma matrix of electrode lead.

Get_DOS(ik=-1)[source]
Inspect_Lorentzian_fit(lead, I, J, i, j, Emin=-3.0, Emax=3.0, ik=0, size=2, center_lines=False, source='sampled_gl', n_samples=100, return_result=False, scipy_peak_opts={}, scipy_peaks=True, return_peaks=False, pinfo_size=8.0, width_nn=2, use_dense_grid=False, logplot=False, add_flipped_poles=False, cl_lims=(None, None), NO=True, unit_x='[eV]', unit_y='[eV]')[source]

This function is meant to compare the fit to the actual sampled Gammas coming from TBtrans.

I,J denotes the overall blocks, the nonzero elements can be found inspecting the .iszero class attribute of the block_sparse and block_td classes. In particular, try e.g. to print .Nonortho_Gammas[0].is_zero if you have already used the read_data command.

i,j are the sub-indices within the particular block chosen by I and J.

The rest of the arguments can be familiarized by experimentation.

Inspect_Poles(lead, ik=0, size=5, marker_L='*', marker_F='.', fermi_poles=True)[source]

Prints a picture of the poles used in the fit. It simply uses the ei and gamma arrays which is attributes in the self.fitted_lorentzians[i] block-sparse matrix classes.

There are some plotting parameters in the function arguments.

Inspect_SE_from_hilbert_transform(lead, I, J, i, j, Emin=-3.0, Emax=3.0, ik=0, size=2, return_result=False, n_samples=200, NO=False)[source]
Inspect_Transmission(i, j, lead=0, kpnt=None, return_result=False, invinp=None, xlims=None)[source]
Inspect_dos_from_hilbert_transform(E=array([-5., -4.8989899, -4.7979798, -4.6969697, -4.5959596, -4.49494949, -4.39393939, -4.29292929, -4.19191919, -4.09090909, -3.98989899, -3.88888889, -3.78787879, -3.68686869, -3.58585859, -3.48484848, -3.38383838, -3.28282828, -3.18181818, -3.08080808, -2.97979798, -2.87878788, -2.77777778, -2.67676768, -2.57575758, -2.47474747, -2.37373737, -2.27272727, -2.17171717, -2.07070707, -1.96969697, -1.86868687, -1.76767677, -1.66666667, -1.56565657, -1.46464646, -1.36363636, -1.26262626, -1.16161616, -1.06060606, -0.95959596, -0.85858586, -0.75757576, -0.65656566, -0.55555556, -0.45454545, -0.35353535, -0.25252525, -0.15151515, -0.05050505, 0.05050505, 0.15151515, 0.25252525, 0.35353535, 0.45454545, 0.55555556, 0.65656566, 0.75757576, 0.85858586, 0.95959596, 1.06060606, 1.16161616, 1.26262626, 1.36363636, 1.46464646, 1.56565657, 1.66666667, 1.76767677, 1.86868687, 1.96969697, 2.07070707, 2.17171717, 2.27272727, 2.37373737, 2.47474747, 2.57575758, 2.67676768, 2.77777778, 2.87878788, 2.97979798, 3.08080808, 3.18181818, 3.28282828, 3.38383838, 3.48484848, 3.58585859, 3.68686869, 3.78787879, 3.88888889, 3.98989899, 4.09090909, 4.19191919, 4.29292929, 4.39393939, 4.49494949, 4.5959596, 4.6969697, 4.7979798, 4.8989899, 5.]), eta=0.0, i=0, j=1, lead=0, kpnt=None, return_result=False)[source]
Inspect_fitted_transmission(Eg, eta=0.001, i=0, j=1, return_result=False, kpnt=None, lead=0)[source]

Not really tested, might be wrong. It seems to be there from when the code was being concieved.

Inspect_transmission_from_hilbert_transform(E=array([-5., -4.8989899, -4.7979798, -4.6969697, -4.5959596, -4.49494949, -4.39393939, -4.29292929, -4.19191919, -4.09090909, -3.98989899, -3.88888889, -3.78787879, -3.68686869, -3.58585859, -3.48484848, -3.38383838, -3.28282828, -3.18181818, -3.08080808, -2.97979798, -2.87878788, -2.77777778, -2.67676768, -2.57575758, -2.47474747, -2.37373737, -2.27272727, -2.17171717, -2.07070707, -1.96969697, -1.86868687, -1.76767677, -1.66666667, -1.56565657, -1.46464646, -1.36363636, -1.26262626, -1.16161616, -1.06060606, -0.95959596, -0.85858586, -0.75757576, -0.65656566, -0.55555556, -0.45454545, -0.35353535, -0.25252525, -0.15151515, -0.05050505, 0.05050505, 0.15151515, 0.25252525, 0.35353535, 0.45454545, 0.55555556, 0.65656566, 0.75757576, 0.85858586, 0.95959596, 1.06060606, 1.16161616, 1.26262626, 1.36363636, 1.46464646, 1.56565657, 1.66666667, 1.76767677, 1.86868687, 1.96969697, 2.07070707, 2.17171717, 2.27272727, 2.37373737, 2.47474747, 2.57575758, 2.67676768, 2.77777778, 2.87878788, 2.97979798, 3.08080808, 3.18181818, 3.28282828, 3.38383838, 3.48484848, 3.58585859, 3.68686869, 3.78787879, 3.88888889, 3.98989899, 4.09090909, 4.19191919, 4.29292929, 4.39393939, 4.49494949, 4.5959596, 4.6969697, 4.7979798, 4.8989899, 5.]), eta=0.0, i=0, j=1, lead=0, NO=True, kpnt=None, figure=None, return_result=False)[source]

Allows for comparison of the transmission when the gammas from the fit is being used, together with the correction parsed to self.Renormalise_H. numpy linalg inv is used for the matrix inversion, so scales pretty badly.

Parameters:

  • E – np.linspace like array of floats in which to calculate the transmission function. Dont add any imaginary part to this.

  • eta – float, value of eta for the broadening used when calculating the Greens function for the system.

  • j (i /) – electrode label, from / to

  • NO – use the nonorthogonal basis (True, False)

  • kpnt – int, specify which k-point you want to calculate the transmission for

  • return_result – True/False, return the calculated transmission in an array.

Make_Contour(sampling, N_F, tol=1e-06, eps=1e-12, pole_mode='JieHu2011', save_pics=False, Emin=None, Emax=None, tol_aaa=None)[source]
samplingarray of shape (N_lead, N_E) and is the line on which the

W_alc is gonna get fitted on.

N_Fis the number of Pade poles of the Fermi-distibution of lead

alpha. (int)

pole_mode : string, JieHu2011 or Croy2009. save_pics : bool, save a plot of the fermi function if true. tol and eps controls when the points are close enough to each other to count as one. “line” contains the points that tbtrans will sample and must be all the needed points for our calculation Here, replicas of the same energy for different leads are counted as one. One contour that combines the samplings given for each lead is given to TBtrans.

PoleAnalysis(lead, tol=0.01, opts={}, Wmax=0.1, decimals=2, ik=0)[source]

Wrapper for SciPy signal analysis toolbox. Finds the location of the peaks in the gamma-matrix elements which are larger than tol using the scipy find_peaks function.

opts is a dictionary which is parsed to the find_peaks function.

Any poles found by this function should have a widths maller than Wmax.

The poles are rounded to the decimal place given by decimal and combined.

Only the ik th k-point is considered.

PoleGuess(lead, NL, Emin, Emax, fact=1.0, tol=0.01, opts={}, Wmax=0.1, decimals=2, cutoff=0.05, ik=0, pole_dist=1.0, pole_fact=1.0, fillmode='adaptive')[source]

opts: is parsed to scipy.signal find_peaks fact: is the coarse grid not at the poles pole_dist: distance is the distance from the coarse poles pole_fact: gets multiplied onto the initial guesses in the narrower pole widths.

Renormalise_H(custom_mat)[source]

custom_mat: np.ndarray of shape (nk, 1, no, no) Here you can give a value to renormalize the Orthogonal Hamiltonian Acts as a rigid shift of the Hermitian part of $Sigma_alpha$

SE_from_lorentzian_fit(E, NO=True)[source]

Compute the self-energy using the fit by a Hilbert transformation E is the E-grid on whihc to calculate the self-energy. NO = True gives the SE in the nonorthogonal basis, while False gives it in the lowdin orthogonalized basis.

adapt_sampling(dist=5.0, dx_coarse=0.25, tol=0.01, opts={}, Wmax=0.1, decimals=2, ik=0, cutoff=0.01)[source]

dist: Wi*dist is the radius from which a dense sampling is kept. Poles: Poles[e] = Ei + 1j*Pi are the positions and widths of the samplings

add_broadening(lead, val, ik=0, idx=None)[source]
add_move_center(lead, val, ik=0, idx=None)[source]
bs2np(A)[source]

Convert a block_sparse matrix class which follows the block pattern of the calculation in the self.Device calculation to a dense matrix. The block_sparse matrix we are trying to convert must match the self._Slices which specifies the block structure.

cite()[source]
curvefit_all(tol, ik=0, fix_L_idx=[])[source]

Runs the curvefit function on all components where the exact gamma is larger than tol. has to be run for each kpoint specified by ik

diagonalise(sorting='abs', fixphase=None)[source]

Here the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the coefficient- matrices are found for use in the expansion of the lessor/greater self energies.

eliminate_small_numbers(small=1e-07)[source]

Use Less memory, its good for your PC.

figures(subE=None, manual_D=None, axes=[0, 1], custom_bp=None, spin=0, bs_lim=(-5, 5), Eg=array([-5., -4.97995992, -4.95991984, -4.93987976, -4.91983968, -4.8997996, -4.87975952, -4.85971944, -4.83967936, -4.81963928, -4.7995992, -4.77955912, -4.75951904, -4.73947896, -4.71943888, -4.6993988, -4.67935872, -4.65931864, -4.63927856, -4.61923848, -4.5991984, -4.57915832, -4.55911824, -4.53907816, -4.51903808, -4.498998, -4.47895792, -4.45891784, -4.43887776, -4.41883768, -4.3987976, -4.37875752, -4.35871743, -4.33867735, -4.31863727, -4.29859719, -4.27855711, -4.25851703, -4.23847695, -4.21843687, -4.19839679, -4.17835671, -4.15831663, -4.13827655, -4.11823647, -4.09819639, -4.07815631, -4.05811623, -4.03807615, -4.01803607, -3.99799599, -3.97795591, -3.95791583, -3.93787575, -3.91783567, -3.89779559, -3.87775551, -3.85771543, -3.83767535, -3.81763527, -3.79759519, -3.77755511, -3.75751503, -3.73747495, -3.71743487, -3.69739479, -3.67735471, -3.65731463, -3.63727455, -3.61723447, -3.59719439, -3.57715431, -3.55711423, -3.53707415, -3.51703407, -3.49699399, -3.47695391, -3.45691383, -3.43687375, -3.41683367, -3.39679359, -3.37675351, -3.35671343, -3.33667335, -3.31663327, -3.29659319, -3.27655311, -3.25651303, -3.23647295, -3.21643287, -3.19639279, -3.17635271, -3.15631263, -3.13627255, -3.11623246, -3.09619238, -3.0761523, -3.05611222, -3.03607214, -3.01603206, -2.99599198, -2.9759519, -2.95591182, -2.93587174, -2.91583166, -2.89579158, -2.8757515, -2.85571142, -2.83567134, -2.81563126, -2.79559118, -2.7755511, -2.75551102, -2.73547094, -2.71543086, -2.69539078, -2.6753507, -2.65531062, -2.63527054, -2.61523046, -2.59519038, -2.5751503, -2.55511022, -2.53507014, -2.51503006, -2.49498998, -2.4749499, -2.45490982, -2.43486974, -2.41482966, -2.39478958, -2.3747495, -2.35470942, -2.33466934, -2.31462926, -2.29458918, -2.2745491, -2.25450902, -2.23446894, -2.21442886, -2.19438878, -2.1743487, -2.15430862, 1.91382766, 1.93386774, 1.95390782, 1.9739479, 1.99398798, 2.01402806, 2.03406814, 2.05410822, 2.0741483, 2.09418838, 2.11422846, 2.13426854, 2.15430862, 2.1743487, 2.19438878, 2.21442886, 2.23446894, 2.25450902, 2.2745491, 2.29458918, 2.31462926, 2.33466934, 2.35470942, 2.3747495, 2.39478958, 2.41482966, 2.43486974, 2.45490982, 2.4749499, 2.49498998, 2.51503006, 2.53507014, 2.55511022, 2.5751503, 2.59519038, 2.61523046, 2.63527054, 2.65531062, 2.6753507, 2.69539078, 2.71543086, 2.73547094, 2.75551102, 2.7755511, 2.79559118, 2.81563126, 2.83567134, 2.85571142, 2.8757515, 2.89579158, 2.91583166, 2.93587174, 2.95591182, 2.9759519, 2.99599198, 3.01603206, 3.03607214, 3.05611222, 3.0761523, 3.09619238, 3.11623246, 3.13627255, 3.15631263, 3.17635271, 3.19639279, 3.21643287, 3.23647295, 3.25651303, 3.27655311, 3.29659319, 3.31663327, 3.33667335, 3.35671343, 3.37675351, 3.39679359, 3.41683367, 3.43687375, 3.45691383, 3.47695391, 3.49699399, 3.51703407, 3.53707415, 3.55711423, 3.57715431, 3.59719439, 3.61723447, 3.63727455, 3.65731463, 3.67735471, 3.69739479, 3.71743487, 3.73747495, 3.75751503, 3.77755511, 3.79759519, 3.81763527, 3.83767535, 3.85771543, 3.87775551, 3.89779559, 3.91783567, 3.93787575, 3.95791583, 3.97795591, 3.99799599, 4.01803607, 4.03807615, 4.05811623, 4.07815631, 4.09819639, 4.11823647, 4.13827655, 4.15831663, 4.17835671, 4.19839679, 4.21843687, 4.23847695, 4.25851703, 4.27855711, 4.29859719, 4.31863727, 4.33867735, 4.35871743, 4.37875752, 4.3987976, 4.41883768, 4.43887776, 4.45891784, 4.47895792, 4.498998, 4.51903808, 4.53907816, 4.55911824, 4.57915832, 4.5991984, 4.61923848, 4.63927856, 4.65931864, 4.67935872, 4.6993988, 4.71943888, 4.73947896, 4.75951904, 4.77955912, 4.7995992, 4.81963928, 4.83967936, 4.85971944, 4.87975952, 4.8997996, 4.91983968, 4.93987976, 4.95991984, 4.97995992, 5.]), eta=0.01, n_gammas=10, folder='publish', scipy_peaks=True, center_lines=False, ik=0, logplot=False, custom_idx=None, Eg_eig=None, loglim=(-5, 3), n_split=10)[source]

Function prints a lot of svg figures to the folder specfied. see keyword arguemnt names and experiment with them to get a feeling for them.

get_H_interpolation_function()[source]

Helper function for a obtaining a smooth interpolation of a series of nonequilibrium calculations. Cannot go outside the bias window obviously when you call the resulting function. Inspect the arguments of the function by using help or by trial.

get_dense_matrices(zero_tol=1e-12)[source]

Prepare the propagation quantities in arrays and get a (bad) initial density matrix.

zero_tol defines the lower cutoff on the cutoff of the eigenvalues in the self-energy decompositions. 1e-12 is a very strict value and even 1e-5 can be entirely reasonable.

get_dense_matrices_purenp(zero_tol=1e-15, create_arrays=True)[source]

Prepare the propagation quantities in arrays and get a (bad) initial density matrix.

Furthermore, the Hamiltonian gets the renormalisation given to the class through self.Renormalise_H added to it.

zero_tol defines the lower cutoff on the cutoff of the eigenvalues in the self-energy decompositions. 1e-12 is a very strict value and even 1e-5 can be entirely reasonable.

get_orbital_values(dx)[source]

We can read in the basis of the siesta calculation in the self.Device calculator object, given it has been run and writes the ion.nc files (see siesta compilation dependencies.)

get_propagation_quantities(use_exact_fermi=False)[source]
less_memory(dtype=<class 'numpy.complex64'>)[source]

Use Less memory, its good for your PC. See the Block_matrices code and the block_td and block_sparse classes to understand this part better. We are simply accessing the elements stored.

make_device(name='Device', solution_method='transiesta', pp_folder='../pp', k=[1, 1, 1], basis='SZ', k_tbtrans=[1, 1, 1], elec_inds=None, overwrite=True, Print=False, mpi='', dm_tol='1.d-7')[source]
make_f(parallel=False, fastmath=False, nogil=False)[source]
make_f_experimental(parallel=False, fastmath=False, nogil=False)[source]
make_f_general(parallel=False, fastmath=False, nogil=False)[source]
make_f_gpu(dtype=<class 'int'>)[source]
make_f_purenp()[source]
pickle(filename, compression='lzma')[source]

Saves calculator to file. compression=None or string (default is lzma)

printLval(lead, I, J, i, j, iL, ik=0)[source]

Prints the value of a particular component of the nonorthogonal lorentzian fit.

read_data(sub_orbital=[], which_k=None, less_memory=True, D_lead_ortho=True)[source]

Reads out the gammas, self-energies for the leads reads the Device Hamiltonian, overlap, Lowdin-orthogonalizers

You can specify which orbitals to retain using the sub_orbital keyword of this function. You need to familiarize yourself with the pivotting scheme used in TBtrans, and map your chosen atoms through the pivot indices if you want to go from the initial specification in the .fdf files to the actual Hamiltonian read into this class.

You can also just include a specific list of k-points using the which_k keyword. Check TBtrans/sisl for documentation for k-point ordering.

As said before the main quantities read by this function are the overlap matrix, Hamiltonian, Gamma matrices and self-energies.

reset_all_fits()[source]

Resets all fits

run_curvefit(lead, I, J, i, j, ik=0, use_dense_grid=True, fix_L_idx=[], lin_ramp_dx=5.0)[source]

Runs scipy curvefit on the component of the nonorthognal Gamma denoted by the tuple(lead,I,J,i,j). Can sometimes give better results than the matrix inversion based method used in the self.Fit function. Uses whatever value is already stored in NO_fitted_lorenzians as an initial guess.

run_device(morestuff=[], where='DEFAULT', tbtrans=True, manual_H=None, manual_k=None)[source]

runs the device calculation You can also do this manually and assign the Device attribute yourself.

run_device_non_eq(Vi, morestuff=[], where='DEFAULT', tbtrans=True, mpi='')[source]

Runs the nonequilibrium calculation with voltages Vi Vi should be given in absolute ascending order with a zero at the starting position

This is not strictly necessary to do unless you want to interpolate the device hamiltonian between voltages

Vi is just an iterable with numbers in it

run_electrodes(manual_H=None, parallel_k=False)[source]

runs the electrode calculations You can also do these steps manually and assign the electrode attribute yourself. (See the docstring of make device)

setLval(lead, I, J, i, j, val, ik=0, iL=None, hermitian=True)[source]

Internal function used in the fitting algorithm. Sets the value of a particular component of the nonorthogonal Lorentzian fit.

tofile(name='TDT', tol=1e-07, create_arrays=False, fixphase=None, sorting='abs', exact_fermi=False)[source]

Wraps a series of steps for obtaining the end file in an easy to use function. specifies the lowest absolute value for the eigenvalues. fixphase has been used for testing purposes and are inconsenquencial. The eigenvalues should we sorted according to obs, as this gives the most dense blocks of matrices.

write_to_file(name='TDT', compressed=True)[source]
Zandpack.TimedependentTransport.dot_3d(A, B)[source]
Zandpack.TimedependentTransport.jit_outer(a, b, res)[source]
Zandpack.TimedependentTransport.one2three(y, nk, no, noT, nlead, Nm, nz)[source]
Zandpack.TimedependentTransport.propagate(g, y0, t_span, atol, rtol, method='RK45')[source]
Zandpack.TimedependentTransport.scipy_ode(f, sig0, psi0, omg0, t0, t_eval, dH, delta_variant, Ixi, dH_given=True, method='RK45', dt_guess=None, atol=1e-06, rtol=0.0001)[source]
class Zandpack.TimedependentTransport.sisl_replica(s)[source]

Bases: object

toASE()[source]
to_sisl()[source]
Zandpack.TimedependentTransport.three2one(sig, psi, omg)[source]

Zandpack.Help module

Created on Fri Oct 4 14:21:17 2024

@author: investigator

class Zandpack.Help.TDHelper(Dir, orthogonal=True, valid_ranks=[0])[source]

Bases: object

bare_H0(orthogonal=False)[source]

The Hamiltonian without the lead-device orthogonalisation terms and the correction to H (from using the Renormalise_H function in the TD_Transport class). The Hamiltonian that comes out of this function with orthogonal=False is directly comparible with the device-part of the Hamiltonian from a DFT-calculation. If a linear expansion of the Hamiltonian is done, this is useful.

orthogonal indicates if the the output is represented in the orthogonal basis or not (True or False).

inv_lowdin_transform(A)[source]

returns S^(1/2) @ A @ S^(1/2)

lead_dev_dyncorr(DeltaList=None, orthogonal=True)[source]
lowdin_transform(A)[source]

returns S^(-1/2) @ A @ S^(-1/2)

print_help()[source]
Zandpack.Help.check_for_new_dm()[source]
Zandpack.Help.check_for_stop()[source]
Zandpack.Help.get_SigCorr(ArrayDir)[source]

ArrayDir: String with a path to an Arrays/ subfolder created by Zandpack

Zandpack.Help.remove_NEWH()[source]
Zandpack.Help.signal_new_H()[source]
Zandpack.Help.signal_new_dm()[source]
Zandpack.Help.wait_for_new_H()[source]

Zandpack.FittedSelfEnergy module

Created on Sun Jul 17 22:00:13 2022

@author: aleksander

class Zandpack.FittedSelfEnergy.Lorentzian_SE(Ei, Gi, Coeffs)[source]

Bases: object

evaluate(E, bias=0.0, tol=1e-14, ik=0, hermitian_parts=False)[source]

E: Complex (n,) np.ndarray bias: float, shifting E

evaluate_gamma(E, bias=0.0, tol=1e-15, force_hermitian=False)[source]
Zandpack.FittedSelfEnergy.from_blockmatrix(BM, slices)[source]

BM: Block matrix with lorentzian flag slices: slices for translating block matrix to normal numpy array. This can be taken from either the TimedependentTransport class or the system BTD BM

Zandpack.FittedSelfEnergy.from_eigendecomp(vals, vecs, ivecs, out)[source]
Zandpack.FittedSelfEnergy.from_saved_file(directory, ik=None, print_nonherm_warning=True)[source]

directory: A saved directory containing the needed quantities for the propagation scheme, plus the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the coefficient matrix. See the code TimedepedentTransport.Writer for the needed quantities.

returns: instances of the class Lorentzian_SE for each lead.

Zandpack.FittingTools module

class Zandpack.FittingTools.FitLog[source]

Bases: object

get_previous_fit(i=-1)[source]
save(TDT)[source]
Zandpack.FittingTools.f_space(f, x0, x1, dx)[source]
Zandpack.FittingTools.file_from_lorentzian(Ei, Gi, name='L_file', decimals=8, min_tol=None)[source]
Zandpack.FittingTools.find_correction(R, Emin=-10000.0, Emax=10000.0, linear_part=False, Ei=0.0, Wi=3.0)[source]
Zandpack.FittingTools.lorentzian_from_file(fname)[source]
Zandpack.FittingTools.piecewise_linspace(Ev, n)[source]
Zandpack.FittingTools.rattle_lorentzians(L, mag_E=0.1, mag_G=0.1, ik=None, il=None, seed=None)[source]

Random nudge of the Lorentzian L L: Lorentzian Block-matrix mag_E: positive number mag_G: postive number ik: number or numpy-indices for the k-points to rattle il: indices of the particular lorentzian linewidth function to rattle seed: int for numpy.random.seed

Zandpack.LanczosAlg module

Zandpack.LanczosAlg.Av(M, v)[source]
Zandpack.LanczosAlg.GrSch_last_and_add(Vecs, new)[source]

Vecs: (N, no) or (nk, N, no) new : (no,) or (nk, no) out: (N+1, no) or (nk, N+1, no), Vecs, but with an orthonormalised “new” added

Zandpack.LanczosAlg.Lanczos(M: ndarray, N: int, v_init=None, seed=None)[source]

M: (nk, no,no) or (no,no) N: how many Lanczos vectors v returns : diagonal ai, coupling bi and orthogonal basis

Zandpack.LanczosAlg.Lanczos_eigv(M, N)[source]
Zandpack.LanczosAlg.Norm2(v)[source]
Zandpack.LanczosAlg.dag(v)[source]
Zandpack.LanczosAlg.normalise1(v)[source]
Zandpack.LanczosAlg.normalise2(v)[source]
Zandpack.LanczosAlg.prod(u, v)[source]
Zandpack.LanczosAlg.proj(u, v)[source]

Zandpack.Linalg_factorisation module

Created on Wed Feb 1 11:02:44 2023

@author: aleksander

Zandpack.Linalg_factorisation.EIG(M, Hermitian=False, equality_check=True)[source]
Zandpack.Linalg_factorisation.LDL(M, hermitian=False, equality_check=True)[source]
Zandpack.Linalg_factorisation.QR(M, hermitian=False, equality_check=True)[source]
Zandpack.Linalg_factorisation.SVD(M, hermitian=False, equality_check=True)[source]

Returns svd decomposition in format such that for i in range(num_sing): M[…,:,:] += (U[…,i,:,None]@V[…,i,None,:]) * s[…,i][…,None,None]

Zandpack.Linalg_factorisation.TAKAGI(M, hermitian=False, zero_tol=1e-14, equality_check=True)[source]

Paper:

Singular value decomposition for the Takagi factorization of symmetric matrices Alexander M. Chebotarev a,⇑,1, Alexander E. Teretenkov

Computes Takagi factorisation on giving a matrix on the form A = UDU^T

Zandpack.Louivillian module

Created on Mon Jan 30 09:30:53 2023

@author: aleksander

Zandpack.Louivillian.INNER(M1, M2)[source]
class Zandpack.Louivillian.Louivillian(*args, **kwargs)[source]

Bases: Louivillian

class_type = jitclass.Louivillian#7f7a1c3d8590<H:array(complex128, 2d, A),eig_e:array(float64, 1d, A),eig_v:array(complex128, 2d, A),shape:array(int32, 1d, A),Leig_v:array(complex128, 4d, A),Leig_v_calc:int8>
Zandpack.Louivillian.dag(v)[source]
Zandpack.Louivillian.outer(a, b)[source]

Zandpack.Optimized_RK45 module

Zandpack.Pulses module

Zandpack.Pulses.env1(x)[source]
Zandpack.Pulses.generic_pulse(t, s, f, phase, delay, envelope)[source]

Args: t: time s: abruptness of turning on f: frequency in where it should conform with the time being in femtoseconds (fixed by hbar) phase: phase-delay of sine factor delay: delay for max value of pulse envelope: envelope function, eg the function env, but customizable

Zandpack.Quasiparticle module

Created on Mon Jul 18 14:48:43 2022

@author: aleksander

SteadyState: Simple calculator object for calculation of steadystate properties

Berry Phase functions in the bottom are for calculating berry phases.

Zandpack.Quasiparticle.BerryPhase_1state(v)[source]
Zandpack.Quasiparticle.BerryPhase_Nstate(V)[source]
class Zandpack.Quasiparticle.SteadyState(Ham, SEs)[source]

Bases: object

Current(eta, dH=None, mu_i=[0.0, 0.0], kT_i=[0.025, 0.025], dE_bottom=-40, dE_top=40.0, epsabs=1e-08, epsrel=1e-10)[source]
Glesser(eta, dH=None, mu_i=[0.0, 0.0], kT_i=[0.025, 0.025], dE_bottom=-40, dE_top=40.0, epsabs=1e-06, epsrel=1e-05, N_poles=None, use_FP_theorem=True, n_workers=4)[source]
eq_density_matrix(eta, dH=None, kT=0.025, mu=0.0, dE_bottom=-40, dE_top=1.0, n_workers=1, epsabs=0.0001, epsrel=0.001)[source]
get_shift(N_target, dH=None, eta=0.001, kT=0.025, mu=0.0, dE_bottom=-40, dE_top=1, n_workers=1, method='nelder-mead', x0=0.0, epsabs=0.0001, epsrel=0.001)[source]
solve_QP_equation(E_guess, its, Biases, dHam, Hermitian=True, conv_tol=1e-05)[source]

E_guess: float, starting energy its : int, number of iterations to make eta : add broadening to the self energy evaluation Hermititan: use or dont use np.linalg.eigh biases: defaults to 0.0 for all electrodes, but can be specified as a list/np.array

transmission_and_dos(Egrid, eta, Biases, dH)[source]

Zandpack.Response module

Created on Wed Jan 17 17:26:43 2024

@author: aleksander

get_weights(t)[source]

Zandpack.Response.phi(Ti, T, i, ti)[source]

Zandpack.docstrings module

Zandpack.k0nfig module

GPU: Depricated flag. NUMBA: Use numba at (set true) NUMBA_PARALLEL: set parallel flag in numba decorator to true NUMBA_OUTER… Parallel impl. of outer subtraction. set to true if you use mixed paralllelism CACHE: Set false unless you really want a fast startup time. Downside is you can get a function that has been compiled for another cpu Parallel_PI: Set to True PI: NUMBA OR NUMPY. NUMBA is faster MPI_LAZY_OMEGA: Depricated flag. Supress_Parallel_k: change behavior of splitting of k-points FASTMATH: numba flag. RK_Method: RK45, RK78, DOP54 (RKF45, RKF78, DOPRI5). See mpi_RK4pars_dev.py for more info

Zandpack.mpi_RK4pars module

Created on Sat Feb 19 20:16:48 2022

@author: aleksander

Zandpack.mpi_RK4pars_dev module

Created on Sat Feb 19 20:16:48 2022

@author: aleksander

A is the fractional sampling positions B is the Butcher table CH the new guess of the solution y(ti) CT is the vector giving the error The format assumes an array where the function evaluations are sorted as

Arr_sampling = [k1,k2,…, k_{RK_N, y0]

Zandpack.mpi_RK4pars_dev.hnew_func(h, TE, eps)[source]

Also adds the term Add under the loop, and is optimized to not touch the zeros in omega

Zandpack.mpi_funcs.OuterSubtraction_hard_opti_V2_NOADD_fpole_opti(A, B, C, out, fp_loc, fp_all)[source]

Also adds the term Add under the loop, and is optimized to not touch the zeros in omega

Zandpack.mpi_funcs.hermitian_kaij2ravel(mat, out)[source]
Zandpack.mpi_funcs.nullify_omg_part(A, null_idx, nlrz, i)[source]

Also adds the term Add under the loop, and is optimized to not touch the zeros in omega

Zandpack.mpi_splitter module

Created on Wed May 18 12:06:22 2022

@author: aleksander

Zandpack.mpi_splitter.find_max_orbital_idx_per_lead(GG_P, GL_P, zero_tol, fast=False)[source]

Returns the max orbital index where all following eigenvalues are below the given tolerance. Used in the Zand code

Zandpack.mpi_splitter.get_sources(nw)[source]
Zandpack.mpi_splitter.get_sources_scheme2(nw)[source]
Zandpack.mpi_splitter.partition_scheme2(i, nw, nk, nl, nx)[source]

Splits arrays between mpi-nodes.

If the number of k-points is greater than the number of nodes, the arrays are split the k-index.

Otherwise the arrays are split in the lead and pole indices as The lead is sliced as [0…, N_lead] -> L1=[0,…,N_lead//2], L2=[ N_lead//2, … , N_lead//2] The poles are sliced as [0 … N_poles] -> Xn=[n * (N_poles//(N_nodes/2)), (n+1) * (N_poles//(N_nodes/2))]

node 0:

master node

node 1:

(L1 & L2) and X0

node 2:

L1 and X1

node 3:

L2 and X1

node 4:

L1 and X2

node 5:

L2 and X2

etc…..

Splitting is hardcoded atm and supports: N number of nodes:

N =

2 3 4 7 8 12 16 20 (hpc) 24 (Niflheim) 28 32 (hpc) 40 (Niflheim) 48 56 (Niflheim)

Zandpack.mpi_splitter.partition_scheme3(i, nw, nk, nl, nx, noT_lead, eigsplit=1)[source]
Zandpack.mpi_splitter.partition_scheme_for_k(i, nw, nk, nl, nx)[source]

dirs : the *_save directory/ies where a Zand calculation has been done n : number of electrodes

Zandpack.mpi_tools.combine_dm(dirs, times_label='DMt')[source]

like combine_currents, but for the density matrix instead.

Zandpack.mpi_tools.combine_pi(dirs, times_label='DMt')[source]

like combine_currents, but for the PI matrix instead.
